title: The Top Five Ethan Hawke Movies and a Double Shot of Richard Linklater, With Ethan Hawke and Richard Linklater!
format: Sean Fennessey and Amanda Dobbins review Richard Linklater's two 2025 films: Nouvelle Vague, a black-and-white recreation of Godard shooting Breathless, and Blue Moon, a one-night portrait of washed-up lyricist Lorenz Hart with Ethan Hawke shaved bald and scaled down to five feet. They handicap a crowded Best Actor race, each rank their five favorite Hawke performances, then Sean talks with Hawke and Linklater, who spent more than a decade getting Blue Moon made.

segments.heading: The Governors Awards and the start of Oscar season
segments.note: honorary Oscars, and a Cruise theory

films.title: Bardo, False Chronicle of a Handful of Truths
films.note: Cited as the exception to Iñárritu's usual mix of commercial reach and awards traction; the one that drew neither. Amanda mainly remembers the apartment's tile work.

films.title: Sinners
films.note: Sean's evidence that an April release can still contend, waved off against worries that Cruise's next Iñárritu film landing in summer 2026 hurts its Oscar odds. Tom Cruise took an honorary Oscar this night, a year before that bid; Sean wants him to get a real one.

segments.heading: The live-action 'Moana' teaser
segments.note: another Disney remake nobody asked for

films.title: Moana
films.note: Turns ten in 2026, still in heavy rotation in the Fennessey house, which is why a remake this soon feels odd. Sean can perform 'How Far I'll Go' start to finish and rates it a masterpiece.

films.title: Moana 2
films.note: The sequel arrived just last year, part of why the property never left the culture long enough to need reviving.

films.title: Avatar: The Way of Water
films.note: What the live-action Moana teaser looked like to both of them: the island, the water, the creatures. It reads as animated anyway.

films.title: The Lion King
films.note: The recent benchmark for the largest gap between original and remake, roughly 25 years, unlike Moana's ten.

films.title: Beauty and the Beast
films.note: Held up alongside Lion King as a remake that at least recreated some 'ancient magic'; the Moana redo doesn't even have that excuse.

films.title: The Little Mermaid
films.note: Moana as heir to Ariel's break-from-home journey, minus the man and the covetousness, which is part of why it lands as one of the good Disney films.

films.title: Zootopia 2
films.note: On track to be one of the year's biggest movies, and a problem for the schedule: where does a Zootopia 2 conversation even fit.

films.title: Sense and Sensibility
films.note: Ang Lee's version, Emma Thompson's Oscar-winning script and lead, with Kate Winslet, Hugh Grant, Alan Rickman and Hugh Laurie. Getting a 30th-anniversary theatrical rerelease in December; the new Georgia Oakley adaptation with Daisy Edgar-Jones is dated for next September and, per set photos, staying in period. Amanda recommends Thompson's out-of-print production diaries.

films.title: Blue Jean
films.note: Georgia Oakley's previous film, which made Sean expect a modern reimagining of Austen rather than a straight period piece.

films.title: Hedda
films.note: Nia DaCosta's reinterpretation of Hedda Gabler, which Amanda started at 7:30 a.m.; held up as the kind of reimagining the Sense and Sensibility redo apparently isn't.

films.title: Pride & Prejudice
films.note: Joe Wright's film, whose most famous prior adaptation was a TV series, so the format itself felt new; the Sense and Sensibility redo is film-to-film, which Amanda calls trickier.

films.title: Clueless
films.note: An update of Emma, cited alongside Bridget Jones as proof Austen gets remade and reworked constantly.

films.title: Bridget Jones's Diary
films.note: An update of Pride and Prejudice, part of the case that no single Austen text is too sacred to redo.

films.title: Belfast
films.note: Invoked so Sean could note how good Caitríona Balfe and Jamie Dornan look in it; Balfe is set to play the widowed Mrs. Dashwood, which makes both hosts feel old.

films.title: The English Patient
films.note: The one from their movie-swap that Sean was more down on; brought up while sorting out who liked what.

films.title: Nouvelle Vague
films.note: Shot on Kodak black-and-white in period aspect ratio, down to how the subtitles sit on screen, so it plays like a film from 1959. A day-by-day account of Godard on the verge of shooting Breathless, never showing a frame of the finished film. The hangout scenes (the Cahiers office, shit-talking a screening) are the best of it, 'Everybody Wants Some!! for the New Wave'; the actual making-of stunts a bit, and Zoë Deutch as Jean Seberg speaking French in an American accent is, per Amanda, implausible and grating. Guillaume Marbeck's Godard resemblance carries it. Premiered at Cannes, streaming on Netflix in the U.S.

films.title: Breathless
films.note: Godard's film, the framework the movie recreates without ever showing. Already so well-chronicled that Sean found the recreation a little stunty; he rewatched it (streaming on HBO Max) to situate the new film.

films.title: Me and Orson Welles
films.note: The last time Linklater dropped into a period to dramatize a great artist at work, written by the same duo, Holly Gent and Vincent Palmo.

films.title: Everybody Wants Some!!
films.note: Sean's touchstone for the parts that work: friends hanging out, kicking ideas around, needling each other, the mode nobody does better than Linklater.

segments.heading: 'Blue Moon'
segments.note: Ethan Hawke as Lorenz Hart, one night at Sardi's

films.title: Blue Moon
films.note: One of Sean's favorite films of the year. 1943, opening night of Oklahoma!: lyricist Lorenz Hart (Hawke, head shaved, combed over, scaled to five feet, and, per Sean, desperate and unloved) waits at Sardi's for his old partner Richard Rodgers (Andrew Scott). An all-in-one-place movie shot unfussily in 15 days, written by Robert Kaplow. Margaret Qualley plays a Yale student Hart pines for; E.B. White and a young Stephen Sondheim wander through. The rain-fall prologue is the one thing both hosts wish weren't there.

films.title: Casablanca
films.note: Quoted between the characters in the opening ('nobody ever loved me that much'), setting the film's love of old Hollywood right in the text.

films.title: Apollo 10½: A Space Age Childhood
films.note: The rotoscope-animated memory piece, part of a very productive recent run: this, then Hit Man, then Nouvelle Vague and Blue Moon in the same year.

films.title: Hit Man
films.note: Last year's Netflix film, another entry in Linklater's late-career streak. He's made 20-plus movies and, per Sean, is killing it.

films.title: Boyhood
films.note: The reference point for his durational projects, now extended to Merrily We Roll Along, filming with Paul Mescal and Ben Platt through 2040.

segments.heading: The Best Actor race
segments.note: handicapping a crowded year for Hawke

films.title: Blue Moon
films.note: Ethan Hawke. Variety's Clayton Davis had him No. 1 in late September; Sean doesn't think he's in pole position but hopes for a nomination and would love the Gold Derby fifth-place slot to hold.

films.title: One Battle After Another
films.note: Leonardo DiCaprio, whom both treat as a near-lock.

films.title: Marty Supreme
films.note: Timothée Chalamet, another of the names they see as close to guaranteed.

films.title: The Secret Agent
films.note: Wagner Moura, in the guaranteed tier for both, though neither has seen it yet.

films.title: Sinners
films.note: Michael B. Jordan, the fourth of their locks.

films.title: Springsteen: Deliver Me from Nowhere
films.note: Jeremy Allen White, in the Variety top ten; Amanda can't believe they're still doing this.

films.title: Rental Family
films.note: Brendan Fraser, on the list, unseen by either host.

films.title: The Smashing Machine
films.note: Dwayne Johnson, ranked in both the Variety and AwardsWatch lists they read out.

films.title: Bugonia
films.note: Jesse Plemons in the Best Actor conversation; Sean also flags Emma Stone as a standout in it.

films.title: Jay Kelly
films.note: George Clooney, holding the No. 10 slot on the Variety list.

films.title: Nuremberg
films.note: Russell Crowe, one of the lower-tier names, and a title bound for their upcoming ten-movies catch-up episode.

films.title: Kiss of the Spider Woman
films.note: Raised in the trailer-reel rundown; a pass for Amanda, with respect to Jennifer Lopez.

films.title: The Choral
films.note: Among the Sony Pictures Classics-style trailers stacked before Sean's Blue Moon screening.

films.title: Sirât
films.note: Sean is going in blind and asks Amanda to tell him nothing.

films.title: Ballad of a Small Player
films.note: On Netflix; Sean threw it on and calls it a disappointment.

films.title: The Lost Bus
films.note: Matthew McConaughey, filed under movies they still mean to get to.

segments.heading: Amanda's Top Five Ethan Hawke Movies
segments.subject: Ethan Hawke

films.title: Before Sunset
films.note: No. 1. 'You go by and you go by.'

films.title: Reality Bites
films.note: No. 2. The one she saw at 11 and decided would haunt her for 35 years.

films.title: Boyhood
films.note: No. 3. Not her favorite Linklater, but the clearest study of Hawke over time; divorced-dad energy she's never seen rendered so plainly. Picking up Ellar Coltrane in the muscle car gives her sad chills.

films.title: Blue Moon
films.note: No. 4. A high mark for her, and Hawke trying something outside his range.

films.title: Dead Poets Society
films.note: No. 5. How she first remembers him, and the openness that runs through everything except his asshole-in-his-20s stretch.

segments.heading: Sean's Top Five Ethan Hawke Movies
segments.subject: Ethan Hawke

films.title: First Reformed
films.note: No. 1. Paul Schrader; a movie Sean says is on the money about how everything feels now, and that he should have fought for in the '25 for 25' rankings. Hawke as another desperate man who's lost hope.

films.title: Before Sunset
films.note: No. 2. The signature of the Before trilogy's map-your-own-life quality.

films.title: Before the Devil Knows You're Dead
films.note: No. 3. Sidney Lumet's crime film, Hawke magic opposite Philip Seymour Hoffman as two very different brothers.

films.title: Blue Moon
films.note: No. 4. Matched with Amanda at four so they could line up their lists.

films.title: Training Day
films.note: No. 5. Big Hollywood movie star and actor's workout at once; Sean argues Denzel's performance wouldn't land the same opposite a lesser actor.

segments.heading: What makes Ethan Hawke great
segments.note: the phases of a career, five years ahead of Sean

films.title: Reality Bites
films.note: The puppy-dog, cooler-than-cool early Hawke; the register Amanda first fell for.

films.title: Hamlet
films.note: The modern-dress adaptation where the '90s-into-'00s Hawke pulls all the way back and plays it cool.

films.title: Training Day
films.note: The 2000s swing back to jitter, a person having a hard time with nothing settled, right on the surface.

films.title: Gattaca
films.note: Sean's proof that Hawke never disappears into makeup; even in the year 2300 he still looks like Ethan Hawke.

segments.heading: In conversation: Ethan Hawke and Richard Linklater
segments.guest: Ethan Hawke and Richard Linklater on

films.title: Blue Moon
films.note: Kaplow handed Linklater the script around 2012, before Boyhood wrapped; they read it aloud over years whenever all three were in the same city. Hawke describes shifting from the confident tall guy to a five-foot man nobody ever wanted, 'rhythm guitar to violin,' with Linklater nagging the physical transformation until Hawke disappeared. Shot in 15 days off long unbroken takes, the way they learned to work on Tape.

films.title: Boyhood
films.note: The last time the two shot a film together before Blue Moon, a 10-year gap; also Linklater's model for tracking one actor across time.

films.title: Before Sunrise
films.note: Hawke's line that there were maybe five fans on earth waiting for a sequel, so a nine-year wait cost nothing; 'the least successful film to ever spawn a sequel.'

films.title: Tape
films.note: Their earlier single-location, small-cast film, shot in six days; the training that made Blue Moon's long takes possible.

films.title: Me and Orson Welles
films.note: Based on Robert Kaplow's novel, though Kaplow didn't write that screenplay; Blue Moon is his first.

films.title: The Graduate
films.note: The Mike Nichols story Hawke tells about why Redford couldn't play the lead, because he'd never known a girl to turn him down, which is exactly the self-loathing Hawke had to reach for as Hart.

films.title: The American Friend
films.note: Hawke's last-great pick: Wim Wenders, Robbie Müller's photography he calls stunning, Dennis Hopper, some plot holes he'd have loved to punch up.

films.title: The Story of Adèle H.
films.note: Linklater's recent rewatch, still just as beautiful; part of the Truffaut thread that runs under both his new films.

films.title: Small Change
films.note: The Truffaut film Noah Baumbach recommended to Linklater the day before; 'Truffaut never goes away.'

films.title: One Battle After Another
films.note: The current film both single out, and Hawke's occasion to marvel at DiCaprio's run and at DiCaprio opposite Paul Thomas Anderson's cast.

films.title: Bugonia
films.note: Hawke on Emma Stone: 'who is this woman,' great and nonstop.

films.title: One Flew Over the Cuckoo's Nest
films.note: Hawke's boyhood example of art as invitation, not instruction, the chief running into the woods and the mystery he's still turning over.

Bardo, False Chronicle of a Handful of T.title: BARDO, False Chronicle of a Handful of Truths

Before the Devil Knows You're Dead.title: Before the Devil Knows You're Dead

One Flew Over the Cuckoo's Nest.title: One Flew Over the Cuckoo's Nest
